era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is consolidating in Northern Europe; the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire has just been completed (1521); the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman is expanding in Eastern Europe and the Mediterranean; the Ming Dynasty is in its mid-reign period; and European exploration of the Americas is accelerating. This is a period of religious upheaval, imperial consolidation, and global exploration.

events.title: Magellan's expedition completes the first circumnavigation of the globe
events.description: Ferdinand Magellan's expedition, which departed Spain in 1519, completes the first circumnavigation of the globe when the surviving ship, the Victoria, returns to Spain under the command of Juan Sebastián Elcano on September 6, 1522. Although Magellan himself was killed in the Philippines in 1521, his expedition proved that the world was larger than previously believed and that a westward route to Asia was possible. The voyage lasted nearly three years, and of the original five ships and approximately 270 men, only one ship and 18 men returned. The expedition's findings revolutionized European understanding of global geography and opened new possibilities for trade and exploration.
key_figures: Juan Sebastián Elcano (Spanish navigator, expedition commander on return)

sources.name: Antonio Pigafetta's account of the voyage (eyewitness chronicle)
Spanish royal records and contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: Magellan's circumnavigation is one of the most well-documented voyages of the age. The return date of September 6, 1522, is firmly established in contemporary records.

events.title: Pope Adrian VI elected; beginning of his brief papacy
events.description: Adrian VI (born Adrian Florenszoon) is elected Pope in January 1522, following the death of Pope Leo X. Adrian VI is the last non-Italian pope before the modern era and is known for his attempts to reform the Church and address the challenges posed by the Protestant Reformation. His papacy is brief (he dies in 1523) but significant for his efforts to combat corruption and his diplomatic attempts to reconcile with Protestant reformers. His election represents a moment of hope for Church reform, though his efforts are ultimately limited by his short tenure and the entrenched interests of the Roman Curia.
events.certainty_note: Adrian VI's election and papacy are well-documented in papal records and contemporary sources. The date and significance are firmly established.

events.title: Knights Hospitaller lose Rhodes to Ottoman forces
events.description: After a six-month siege, the Knights Hospitaller, a Christian military order, surrender the island of Rhodes to Ottoman forces under Süleyman the Magnificent. The siege, which began in July 1522, ends with the Knights' evacuation in December 1522. The loss of Rhodes marks the end of the Knights' control of the eastern Mediterranean and represents a major Ottoman victory in the region. The Knights subsequently relocate to Malta, where they establish a new base. The fall of Rhodes is a symbolic and strategic victory for the Ottoman Empire and demonstrates Süleyman's military prowess.
key_figures: Grand Master Philippe Villiers de l'Isle-Adam (Knights Hospitaller)

sources.name: European accounts of the siege, including those by the Knights

events.certainty_note: The siege of Rhodes is extensively documented in both Ottoman and European sources. The dates and outcome are firmly established.

events.title: Cortés establishes the capital of New Spain at Mexico City
events.description: Following the fall of Tenochtitlan in 1521, Hernán Cortés establishes Mexico City as the capital of the new Spanish colony of New Spain. The city is built on the ruins of Tenochtitlan, the former Aztec capital, and becomes the administrative and economic center of Spanish colonial rule in Mexico. The establishment of Mexico City marks the transition from conquest to colonial administration and the beginning of the systematic reorganization of indigenous territories under Spanish rule. The city becomes one of the largest and most important cities in the Spanish colonial empire.

sources.name: Spanish colonial records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: The establishment of Mexico City as the capital of New Spain is well-documented in Spanish colonial records and Cortés's accounts. The date and significance are firmly established.

events.title: Peasants' War in the Holy Roman Empire continues to escalate
events.description: The Peasants' War, which began in 1524 (note: this is a chronological error in the prompt; the Peasants' War began in 1524, not 1522), is not yet occurring in 1522. However, the social and economic tensions that will lead to the Peasants' War are building in 1522. The Protestant Reformation, initiated by Martin Luther in 1517, is spreading and radicalizing, and peasants are beginning to interpret Lutheran theology as justifying social and economic reform. The year 1522 sees increasing social unrest and the emergence of radical preachers who will lead the Peasants' War.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the preconditions of the Peasants' War

events.certainty_note: The social tensions of 1522 are documented in contemporary sources, though the major outbreak of the Peasants' War occurred in 1524-1526. This entry documents the preconditions.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Magellan's circumnavigation as a triumph of European exploration has been complicated by modern scholarship, which emphasizes the indigenous peoples encountered, the violence of the expedition, and the limited immediate impact of the voyage on European trade and knowledge. Additionally, the loss of Rhodes is sometimes portrayed as a decisive Ottoman victory, but modern scholarship emphasizes the Knights' continued importance in Mediterranean politics and their successful relocation to Malta. The establishment of Mexico City is sometimes presented as a straightforward transfer of power, but modern scholarship emphasizes the destruction of indigenous culture and the complex processes of colonial reorganization.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1522 is rich for European events, particularly the circumnavigation and the loss of Rhodes, which are extensively recorded in contemporary sources. However, the perspective is heavily European. Indigenous perspectives on the establishment of Mexico City are preserved primarily in later compilations and codices. Ottoman records are detailed but may emphasize imperial achievements. The narrative of the Reformation emphasizes religious and intellectual history, potentially underrepresenting economic and social factors. The social tensions that will lead to the Peasants' War are documented in contemporary sources, but the full significance of these tensions may not have been apparent to contemporaries.

graph_edges.note: Magellan's circumnavigation completed the voyage that departed in 1519
The expedition continued after Magellan's death in 1521 and returned in 1522
Adrian VI's election occurred during the early stages of the Protestant Reformation
The loss of Rhodes was part of Süleyman's broader expansion in the Mediterranean, following the Siege of Belgrade
The establishment of Mexico City followed the fall of Tenochtitlan
